did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically a custom-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for melanoma classification and DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation, on the HAM10000 dermatology dataset to obtain results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2023 IEEE EMBC conference. It describes the development of a novel CNN architecture and the use of DeepLabV3+ for segmentation, followed by empirical evaluation on the HAM10000 dataset with reported metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.). It is not a review or survey.

title: Analyzing the Impact of Image Denoising and Segmentation on Melanoma Classification Using Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2023 45th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine & Biology Society (EMBC)
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of image denoising and segmentation on melanoma classification using a custom-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on the HAM10000 dataset. The authors employed bilateral filtering for noise reduction and DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation to preprocess the dermoscopic images. The classification network was trained and evaluated on raw, denoised, and denoised+segmented data subsets. Experimental results demonstrated that incorporating pre-processing and segmentation steps improved performance, with the denoised+segmented data achieving the highest accuracy of 93.40%, precision of 93.45%, and recall of 94.51%. The findings conclude that while deep learning can process raw data, pre-processing significantly enhances classification metrics by removing image complexities.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (melanoma vs. benign). The study focuses on melanoma classification using the HAM10000 dataset.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The HAM10000 dataset comprises two classes: benign and malignant. Specific benign subtypes mentioned in the context of the dataset's composition or related literature include nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of benign lesion and in the conclusion as a lesion type with color variations. Not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the reported results.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of benign lesion. Not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the reported results.
Mentioned in the conclusion as a lesion type with color variations. Not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the reported results.
Mentioned in the conclusion as a lesion type with color variations. Not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the reported results.

all_task_types.details: The paper develops a custom CNN for binary classification of melanoma versus benign lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs probabilities via a softmax layer and is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score. While DeepLabV3+ is used for segmentation (producing binary masks), the primary predictive task of the novel network presented is classification.

DeepLabV3+ is employed to produce a binary segmentation map separating the lesion region from the background. This is used as a preprocessing step for the classification task rather than the primary end-to-end prediction task of the study's novel contribution.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, utilized as the benchmark dataset for the experimental study.

use.training: The dataset was resized to 227x227, augmented using rotation, scaling, and translation to balance classes and resolve overfitting, and then used to train the custom CNN on raw, denoised, and denoised+segmented subsets.
use.testing: The dataset (specifically the test sets derived from the processed subsets) was used to evaluate the classification network's performance using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-score.
use.other: The dataset was also used to generate denoised images via bilateral filtering and segmented images via DeepLabV3+ for comparative analysis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between raw, denoised, and denoised+segmented data were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F-score) without statistical significance testing.

Raw Data.metric: ACC%, PRE%, REC%, SPE%, F-Score%, PSNR (DB)
Raw Data.notes: Results for the raw dataset baseline.

Denoised Data.metric: ACC%, PRE%, REC%, SPE%, F-Score%, PSNR (DB)
Denoised Data.notes: Results for the denoised dataset using bilateral filtering.

Segmented Data.metric: ACC%, PRE%, REC%, SPE%, F-Score%, PSNR (DB)
Segmented Data.notes: Results for the denoised+segmented dataset using DeepLabV3+. This configuration achieved the highest performance metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classification; DeepLabV3+ for segmentation
architecture_used.pretraining: DeepLabV3+ was pre-trained and then fine-tuned. The custom classification CNN was designed from scratch (no pretraining reported).

classification_network.structure: Six sequential blocks with identical layer configuration but varying kernel counts, followed by three fully connected layers.
block_layers: Convolutional layer (3x3 filter, stride/padding for boundary alignment)
classification_network.dropout: Applied before fully connected layers to reduce overfitting

segmentation_network.backbone: 101 backbone layers forming a deep network with residual connections
segmentation_network.key_mechanism: Atrous convolution (dilation rates) instead of pooling layers to maintain spatial resolution and broaden receptive field
segmentation_network.preprocessing_for_segmentation: Bilateral filtering applied to input images

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (implemented in MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox)
training_details.augmentation: Rotation (-20 to 20 degrees), scaling, translation (-5 to 5)
training_details.hardware: GPU machines with GeForce RTX 2060 and 2080

classification_or_output_head.function: Converts feature vector values to probabilities for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign)
classification_or_output_head.loss_calculation: Cross-entropy loss between predicted probabilities and target labels

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images resized from original 1022x767 to 227x227 using bi-linear interpolation.
Data Augmentation: Online augmentation using rotation (-20 to 20 degrees), scaling, and translation (-5 to 5) to balance classes and resolve overfitting.
Denoising/Contrast Enhancement: Bilateral filtering applied to increase color contrast, smooth images, and reduce noise while preserving edges.
Segmentation: DeepLabV3+ model used to produce binary segmentation masks (region of interest) to eliminate irrelevant pixel information.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Online augmentation with rotation angles in the range of [-20, 20] degrees.
Online augmentation applied to resolve overfitting and balance classes.
Online augmentation with translation factors in the range of [-5, 5].

conclusions: Incorporating pre-processing (denoising via bilateral filtering) and segmentation (using DeepLabV3+) steps significantly improves the classification performance of deep learning models for melanoma detection.

The custom-designed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved its highest performance metrics when trained on denoised and segmented data, with an accuracy of 93.40%, precision of 93.45%, recall of 94.51%, specificity of 92.08%, and F-score of 93.98%.

While deep learning classifiers can process raw data efficiently, pre-processing and segmentation reduce image complexity and irrelevant pixel information, leading to higher accuracy and more relevant predictions.

The denoised and segmented data subsets yielded higher Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) values compared to raw data, indicating improved image quality which contributes to better classification results.

The study acknowledges that future work should utilize larger datasets and employ more efficient noise removal algorithms and segmentation approaches to further enhance classification accuracy for complex lesion samples such as Nevus, Actinic, and Squamous lesions.
important_remarks: The study was not supported by any organization, indicating a lack of external funding or institutional backing.
The custom CNN architecture was designed from scratch rather than using established state-of-the-art backbones (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet), which limits direct comparability with modern benchmarks.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance improvements were reported as descriptive metric differences without significance testing.
The dataset (HAM10000) has a highly skewed class distribution between benign and malignant lesions, which was addressed via augmentation but may introduce bias.

The study acknowledges that complex lesion types such as Nevus, Actinic keratosis, and Squamous cell carcinoma were not explicitly isolated or analyzed separately, limiting generalizability to these specific subtypes.
The work is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The classification network was trained and tested separately on raw, denoised, and denoised+segmented subsets, but the paper does not report whether the segmentation model (DeepLabV3+) was trained on the same data split, posing a potential data leakage risk if segmentation masks were derived from test data.
The study relies on bilateral filtering for denoising, which is a traditional method, and acknowledges that more efficient noise removal algorithms are needed for future work.

The hardware used (GeForce RTX 2060/2080) and software (MATLAB 2021b) are specified, but computational efficiency or inference time metrics are not reported, limiting assessment of clinical deployment feasibility.
